scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE DISTRICT JUDGE / CITY CIVIL COURT
AT BENGALURU

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT RELATING TO IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business / resides within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court.

COMMERCIAL COURTS ACT COMPLIANCE

4. The Plaintiff states that the suit value exceeds the specified value under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The Plaintiff has complied with the mandatory pre-institution mediation process under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, as applicable. {{SECTION_12A_COMPLIANCE_STATUS}}

LIMITATION

5. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from date fixed for performance, or if no date fixed, when plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

FACTS OF THE CASE

6. The Plaintiff agreed to purchase from the Defendant a residential property bearing Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft, more particularly described in the Schedule of Property with Survey No. {{PROPERTY_SURVEY_NUMBERS}} (hereinafter referred to as the "Schedule Property"). Pursuant to this agreement, the Plaintiff and Defendant executed an Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024 for a total consideration of Rs. 85,00,000/-, copy of which is annexed herewith as Annexure A.

7. In terms of the said Agreement to Sell, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant on 10.01.2024 via RTGS bearing UTR No. HDFC20240110987654. The Plaintiff has retained the proof of said payment, copy of which is annexed herewith as Annexure B.

8. As per the terms of the Agreement to Sell, the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was agreed to be paid on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution and registration of the Sale Deed. The Plaintiff has always been ready and willing to perform her part of the contract and pay the balance consideration within the stipulated time.

9. The Plaintiff has complied with all necessary statutory formalities required under the applicable laws for the validity of the Agreement to Sell, including {{STATUTORY_PAYMENT_DETAILS}}. The Plaintiff is entitled to seek specific performance of the said contract as per the terms agreed upon between the parties.

BREACH OF CONTRACT

10. Despite the Plaintiff's readiness and willingness, the Defendant vide letter dated 05.06.2024 communicated his refusal to execute the Sale Deed, citing receipt of a higher offer from a third party, copy of which is annexed herewith as Annexure C. Consequently, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the Sale Deed, copy of which along with postal receipt is annexed herewith as Annexure D.

11. The Defendant has failed and neglected to comply with the legal notice and has refused to execute the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff. The Defendant's actions constitute a clear breach of the contractual obligations undertaken in the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024.

DAMAGES

12. Due to the Defendant's wrongful refusal and delay in performing his contractual obligations, the Plaintiff has suffered financial loss owing to the escalation in the market value of immovable properties in the locality of Koramangala, Bengaluru. The Plaintiff is entitled to be compensated for the loss suffered due to the non-availability of the Schedule Property at the agreed price.

13. The Defendant's conduct has caused significant mental agony, harassment, and financial strain to the Plaintiff, compelling her to institute the present suit. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover the costs of this litigation and any other relief that this Hon'ble Court deems fit to grant in the interests of justice.

LEGAL BASIS

14. The present cause is governed by the Specific Relief Act, 1963, including Section 10 (mandatory after 2018 Amendment), Section 16, Section 21, and Section 22.

15. The present cause is governed by the Indian Contract Act, 1872, including Section 10.

16. The Plaintiff also relies on the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, including Section 53A, where the pleaded facts attract those provisions.

CAUSE OF ACTION

17. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

18. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated 20.06.2024, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

19. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach consisted of a single event. However, the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

20. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

21. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, directing the Defendant to execute and register the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon payment of the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/-;
    (b) In the alternative, or in addition to relief (a), pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for a sum of Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) towards damages for breach of contract;
    (c) Award pendente lite and future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (d) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt with UTR: HDFC20240110987654

Annexure C — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

Annexure D — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru.
Represented by its Director: Priya Menon, aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A limited liability partnership having its office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru.
Represented by its Designated Partner: Vikram Singh, aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

2. The suit is valued above the specified value of Rs. 3,00,000/- as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

3. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

4. This Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business / resides within such territorial limits.

5. This Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial disputes under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

LIMITATION

6. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from when contract is broken; or for continuing breach, when it ceases. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

7. The Plaintiff has complied with the mandatory pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and the certificate of mediation is filed herewith as Annexure F. [OR: The Plaintiff seeks urgent interim relief and is exempt from pre-institution mediation under Section 12A.]

FACTS OF THE CASE

8. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff and Defendant entered into a Software Development Agreement for development of an ERP system for a total contract value of Rs. 45,00,000/-, marked as **Annexure A**.

9. Pursuant to the said Agreement, the Plaintiff paid a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- as the first milestone payment to the Defendant via NEFT on 01.02.2024, bearing UTR Number ICIC20240201456789, marked as **Annexure B**.

10. As per the terms of the Agreement, the Defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system on or before 01.05.2024.

11. The Defendant failed to deliver the said first module by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024.

12. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant abandoned the project without delivering any module, as evidenced by email correspondence marked as **Annexure C**.

13. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 calling upon the Defendant to refund the advance and compensate for losses, marked as **Annexure E**, to which the Defendant has failed to comply.

BREACH PARTICULARS

14. The Defendant breached the terms of the Agreement, specifically Clause regarding delivery timelines, by failing to deliver the first module by the stipulated date of 01.05.2024.

15. The Defendant further breached the Agreement by unilaterally abandoning the project on 15.06.2024 without completing the agreed scope of work.

16. Despite the abandonment and demand made via legal notice, the Defendant has failed to refund the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- received from the Plaintiff.

17. The cause of action for the present suit arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and failed to deliver the module. The cause of action continues to subsist as the Defendant has failed to refund the amount despite notice.

DAMAGES

18. The Plaintiff is entitled to the refund of the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid to the Defendant.

19. Due to the delay in operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system, the Plaintiff suffered consequential losses quantified at Rs. 12,00,000/-, as per the loss assessment report by the auditor marked as **Annexure D**.

20. The Plaintiff claims total damages of Rs. 32,00,000/- comprising Rs. 20,00,000/- towards refund of advance and Rs. 12,00,000/- towards consequential damages.

21. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest at the rate of 18% per annum on the outstanding amount from the date of breach until realisation.

LEGAL BASIS

22. The Defendant has committed a repudiatory breach by refusing to perform its obligations, entitling the Plaintiff to treat the contract as at an end and seek appropriate relief.

23. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it.

CAUSE OF ACTION

24. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

25. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated 01.07.2024, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

26. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach consisted of a single event. However, the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

27. The suit is valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

28. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for a sum of Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) towards damages;
    (b) Award Rs. 20,00,000/- towards Refund of Advance;
    (c) Award Rs. 12,00,000/- towards Consequential loss / damages;
    (d) Award pendente lite and future interest at 18% per annum or such rate as this Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (e) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF DAMAGES / PARTICULARS OF DAMAGES

The Plaintiff claims the following heads of damages with computation methodology:

29. Refund of Advance
   Amount claimed: Rs. 20,00,000/-
   Basis / methodology: First milestone payment made via NEFT (UTR: ICIC20240201456789)
   Documentary proof: Annexure B

30. Consequential loss / damages
   Amount claimed: Rs. 1
